settings.title: A Shaded Courtyard Turned Into a Green Room
settings.summary: We brought light and planting into a dark north-facing courtyard behind a terraced house.
settings.description:
The brief

The courtyard at the back of the Pearsons' terrace got barely two hours of sun and had been left as cracked concrete with a single dying bay tree. It faced north, the surrounding walls were dark brick, and the family had given up using the space at all.

They did not want a paved yard with a few pots. They wanted somewhere they could sit, that read as green rather than grey, and that did not depend on plants needing full sun to survive.

What we did

We lifted the old concrete and laid pale limestone setts across the floor to bounce what little light reached the space. Along two of the walls we built raised beds, which let us bring in good depth of soil where there had only been a thin, compacted layer before.

For planting we leaned on species that genuinely cope with shade rather than just tolerate it. We used ferns and hostas for low foliage and a multi-stem amelanchier as the one piece of height, chosen because it handles a shaded position and earns its place with blossom and autumn colour.

  • Pale limestone setts laid to reflect light into the courtyard
  • Raised beds along two walls for proper soil depth
  • Shade planting of ferns, hostas, and a multi-stem amelanchier
  • Walls left light to keep the space feeling open

The result

The Pearsons now eat breakfast out there most of the year. The combination of white walls and green foliage makes the small footprint feel calm rather than cramped, and a courtyard that had been written off has become the room they use most in good weather.
